title: Order Management — Lifecycle, Processes & Fulfilment
description: Master the order model, business process engine, fulfilment with consignments, returns, and order lifecycle management.

sections.title: The Order Model
sections.content:
An Order in SAP Commerce Cloud is created when a customer completes checkout and the CommercePlaceOrderStrategy converts the cart. Like Cart, the Order type extends AbstractOrder, so it shares the same structural fields: header-level data (code, currency, user, date, totals, status) and a collection of OrderEntry items (each with a product, quantity, price, and status).

Once created, an order is immutable in its core pricing data — the prices, discounts, and taxes are snapshots captured at placement time. Even if product prices change later, the order retains its original values. This is critical for financial accuracy and audit compliance.

The order has several key relationships: it points to the customer (User), the delivery address (AddressModel), the payment info (PaymentInfoModel), the delivery mode, and the store (BaseStore). It also links to consignments (for fulfilment tracking), payment transactions (for payment lifecycle), and optionally return requests.

Each order has an order code — a unique identifier generated by the KeyGenerator at placement time. The code format is configurable (numeric, alphanumeric, with prefix). In integrated landscapes, this code must be mappable to the ERP sales order number for traceability.

Orders are not catalog-version-aware — they reference products by PK (primary key), not by catalog version. This means an order retains its product references even if the product is later removed from the Online catalog.

sections.title: Order Status Model
sections.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud uses a multi-dimensional status model for orders. Rather than a single status field, the order tracks status across several independent dimensions, each representing a different concern.

Order Status (OrderStatus enum) — the overall business status of the order: CREATED, CHECKED_VALID, CHECKED_INVALID, PAYMENT_AUTHORIZED, PAYMENT_CAPTURED, COMPLETED, CANCELLED, and others. This is the status most visible to the customer and the one used for high-level reporting.

Delivery Status (DeliveryStatus) — tracks the shipping state: NOTSHIPPED, PARTSHIPPED, SHIPPED. Updated as consignments move through the fulfilment process.

Payment Status (PaymentStatus) — tracks the payment state: not yet paid, partially paid, paid. Updated as payment transactions are captured.

Export Status — tracks whether the order has been exported to an external system (e.g. ERP). Useful for integration monitoring.

Individual order entries also have their own status fields (OrderEntryStatus), enabling partial fulfilment — one entry can be shipped while another is backordered. This multi-dimensional model avoids the oversimplification of a single linear status and accurately represents real-world order complexity.

Status transitions are typically driven by the business process engine (covered next) or by external system callbacks (e.g. a warehouse management system confirming shipment).

sections.title: The Business Process Engine
sections.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud includes a built-in Business Process Engine that orchestrates asynchronous, multi-step workflows. Order management is the primary use case — the order fulfilment process is modelled as a business process with defined steps, decision points, and wait states.

A business process is defined in an XML process definition file (e.g. order-process.xml). The definition contains: actions (Java classes that execute business logic — each implementing BusinessProcessAction), transitions (outcomes of actions that determine the next step — typically OK, NOK, or WAIT), wait nodes (the process pauses until an external event triggers it to continue), and split/join nodes (for parallel execution paths).

The standard order fulfilment process includes steps like: check order validity, authorise payment, fraud check, send order confirmation email, wait for warehouse confirmation, capture payment, send shipment notification, and complete order. Each step is a separate action class, making the process modular and extensible.

Key concepts: processes are persistent — they survive server restarts because their state is stored in the database. Processes are asynchronous — they run on background threads managed by the task engine, not on the request thread. A wait state means the process is paused in the database and will resume only when an external event (e.g. a warehouse callback) triggers it via BusinessProcessService.triggerEvent().

To customise the order process, you can: add new action steps, remove steps, change transitions, or replace individual action beans with custom implementations. The process definition XML is loaded from your extension, so you can provide a completely custom fulfilment flow while reusing standard action classes where they fit.

sections.title: Consignments & Fulfilment
sections.content:
A Consignment (ConsignmentModel) represents a group of order entries fulfilled together from a single source — typically a warehouse or store location. One order can have multiple consignments, enabling split fulfilment across different warehouses or fulfilment centres.

Each consignment contains ConsignmentEntry items that reference order entries with the quantity being fulfilled. For example, an order with 3 products might produce 2 consignments: one from Warehouse A shipping 2 products, and one from Warehouse B shipping the third. This supports ship-from-store, multi-warehouse, and drop-ship scenarios.

Consignments have their own status lifecycle: READY (created, awaiting pick), PICKUP (ready for customer pickup — click-and-collect), SHIPPED (dispatched), DELIVERED, and CANCELLED. The consignment status feeds back into the order's delivery status — when all consignments are shipped, the order's delivery status becomes SHIPPED.

The sourcing strategy determines which warehouse or point of sale fulfils each order entry. The default strategy assigns all entries to a single warehouse, but implementations commonly override this with sophisticated logic based on stock availability, proximity to the customer, or warehouse capacity. The Warehousing module (part of Order Management) extends this with advanced allocation, reallocation, and decline workflows.

Consignment creation and status updates are typically driven by the business process engine. In integrated landscapes, warehouse management systems (WMS) send status updates back to Commerce Cloud, which triggers process events to advance the consignment and order statuses.

sections.title: Cancellations & Returns
sections.content:
Order cancellation can be full (the entire order) or partial (specific entries or quantities). Cancellation is handled by the OrderCancelService which evaluates whether cancellation is allowed based on the current order and consignment status. An order that has already been shipped cannot be cancelled through the standard cancellation flow — it must go through the returns process instead.

Cancellation follows a request-response pattern: a cancellation request is created, validated (is the order in a cancellable state? are the requested entries/quantities valid?), and then executed. Execution updates order entry quantities, recalculates totals, adjusts payment (refund the authorised amount), and updates order status. If all entries are cancelled, the order status becomes CANCELLED; if only some entries are cancelled, the remaining entries continue through fulfilment.

Return requests handle post-shipment returns. A ReturnRequest is created against an order, specifying which entries and quantities are being returned, the return reason, and the refund method. The return follows its own lifecycle: APPROVAL_PENDING → APPROVED → RECEIVED (goods received at warehouse) → COMPLETED (refund issued).

Each return request can generate a refund — either to the original payment method (payment reversal) or as store credit. The refund amount may differ from the original line item price if partial returns, restocking fees, or return shipping costs apply.

Both cancellation and returns integrate with the business process engine — they trigger process events that advance the order through the appropriate status transitions and handle side effects (email notifications, payment reversals, inventory adjustments).

sections.title: Order Management Customisation & Exam Tips
sections.content:
Order management is one of the most complex areas in Commerce Cloud. Here are the customisation points and exam-relevant details:

Business process customisation. The order fulfilment process is defined in XML and executed by modular action classes. To customise: copy the standard process definition to your extension, modify the flow (add/remove/reorder steps), and implement custom action classes where needed. Each action class is a Spring bean implementing BusinessProcessAction with an execute() method that returns a transition string.

triggerEvent is the glue. When an external system (WMS, payment gateway) needs to advance a waiting process, it calls BusinessProcessService.triggerEvent(eventName). The event name matches a wait node in the process definition, causing the process to resume. Forgetting to trigger an event leaves the process stuck in a wait state — a common debugging scenario.

Sourcing strategies. Override the default sourcing strategy to implement multi-warehouse fulfilment, nearest-warehouse logic, or store-based fulfilment. This is a key differentiator for omnichannel implementations.

Order history vs active orders. Completed orders remain in the database indefinitely unless archived. For high-volume sites, implement an archiving strategy (move old orders to a separate data store) to maintain database performance.

Key exam points: Order extends AbstractOrder (same as Cart). Orders have multi-dimensional status (order status, delivery status, payment status). The business process engine uses XML definitions with action classes, transitions, and wait states. Processes are persistent and asynchronous. Consignments enable split fulfilment. triggerEvent resumes a waiting process. OrderCancelService handles cancellations. ReturnRequest handles post-shipment returns. Sourcing strategies determine warehouse allocation.

questions.text: Once an order is placed in SAP Commerce Cloud, what happens to its pricing data if product prices change later?
options: The order prices update automatically to reflect current prices
The order prices remain as snapshots captured at placement time
The order is flagged for manual price reconciliation
The order is cancelled and recreated with new prices
questions.explanation: Order pricing data is immutable — prices, discounts, and taxes are snapshots captured at placement time. Even if product prices change later, the order retains its original values for financial accuracy and audit compliance.

questions.text: SAP Commerce Cloud uses a single status field to track the overall state of an order.
questions.explanation: Commerce Cloud uses a multi-dimensional status model: OrderStatus (overall business status), DeliveryStatus (shipping state), PaymentStatus (payment state), and ExportStatus (external system sync). Individual order entries also have their own status fields.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the Business Process Engine in order management?
options: To calculate cart totals during checkout
To orchestrate asynchronous, multi-step order fulfilment workflows
To manage product catalog synchronisation
To handle user authentication and session management
questions.explanation: The Business Process Engine orchestrates asynchronous, multi-step workflows — primarily order fulfilment. The process is defined in XML with action steps, transitions, wait states, and split/join nodes, running on background threads.

questions.text: Business processes in SAP Commerce Cloud are persistent — they survive server restarts because their state is stored in the database.
questions.explanation: Process state is persisted to the database, meaning processes survive server restarts. When the server comes back up, processes resume from their last saved state — whether that's a wait node, an action step, or a transition point.

questions.text: How is the order fulfilment process defined in SAP Commerce Cloud?
options: As Java annotations on service classes
As an XML process definition file with actions, transitions, and wait states
As ImpEx scripts that run sequentially
As FlexibleSearch queries triggered by CronJobs
questions.explanation: The fulfilment process is defined in an XML file (e.g. order-process.xml) containing actions (Java classes), transitions (OK/NOK/WAIT), wait nodes, and split/join nodes. This makes the process declarative and modular.

questions.text: A single order can have multiple consignments, enabling split fulfilment across different warehouses.
questions.explanation: One order can produce multiple consignments, each fulfilled from a different source. This supports multi-warehouse, ship-from-store, and drop-ship scenarios where different items are sourced from different locations.

questions.text: What method is used to resume a business process that is paused in a wait state?
options: BusinessProcessService.resumeProcess()
BusinessProcessService.triggerEvent(eventName)
BusinessProcessService.advanceStep()
BusinessProcessService.completeWait()
questions.explanation: BusinessProcessService.triggerEvent(eventName) resumes a process paused at a wait node. The event name must match the wait node's expected event in the process definition. Forgetting to trigger an event leaves the process stuck.

questions.text: Each action step in a business process definition is implemented as a Spring bean that implements the BusinessProcessAction interface.
questions.explanation: Each action is a Spring bean implementing BusinessProcessAction with an execute() method that performs the business logic and returns a transition string (e.g. OK, NOK, WAIT) determining the next step.

questions.text: What determines which warehouse or fulfilment centre ships each order entry?
options: The delivery mode selected by the customer
The sourcing strategy
The product's catalog version
The payment service provider
questions.explanation: The sourcing strategy determines warehouse allocation. The default assigns all entries to one warehouse, but implementations commonly override this with logic based on stock availability, proximity, or warehouse capacity.

questions.text: An order that has already been fully shipped can be cancelled through the standard OrderCancelService.
questions.explanation: Shipped orders cannot be cancelled through the standard cancellation flow. They must go through the returns process instead (ReturnRequest), which handles post-shipment returns with their own lifecycle and refund logic.

questions.text: What is the lifecycle of a return request in SAP Commerce Cloud?
options: CREATED → SHIPPED → DELIVERED → CLOSED
APPROVAL_PENDING → APPROVED → RECEIVED → COMPLETED
REQUESTED → VALIDATED → REFUNDED → ARCHIVED
OPEN → IN_PROGRESS → DONE → DELETED
questions.explanation: The return lifecycle is: APPROVAL_PENDING → APPROVED → RECEIVED (goods received at warehouse) → COMPLETED (refund issued). Each transition can trigger business process events for notifications and payment reversals.

questions.text: Orders in SAP Commerce Cloud are catalog-version-aware and will break if the referenced product is removed from the Online catalog.
questions.explanation: Orders are not catalog-version-aware — they reference products by PK (primary key), not by catalog version. An order retains its product references even if the product is later removed from the Online catalog.

questions.text: Partial cancellation of an order (cancelling some entries while others continue to fulfilment) is:
options: Not supported — cancellation is always all-or-nothing
Supported — the OrderCancelService can cancel specific entries or quantities
Only supported through ImpEx scripts
Only available in B2B commerce, not B2C
questions.explanation: Partial cancellation is fully supported. The OrderCancelService can cancel specific entries or quantities within an order. Remaining entries continue through fulfilment, and the order status reflects the partial cancellation.

questions.text: When all consignments for an order reach SHIPPED status, the order's delivery status automatically updates to SHIPPED.
questions.explanation: Consignment statuses feed back into the order. When all consignments are shipped, the order's DeliveryStatus becomes SHIPPED. If only some are shipped, it becomes PARTSHIPPED. This aggregation happens through the business process engine.
